You said:
fatal: pathspec 'table02.html' did not match any files
ChatGPT said:

That error message comes from Git. It means Git couldn’t find a file named table02.html in your working directory that matches what you typed. Common causes:

  1. File doesn’t exist yet
    Maybe table02.html was never created or got deleted.

  2. File is in the wrong directory
    If you’re in a subfolder, Git is only looking relative to your current path. Try:

    bash
    ls

    to check if table02.html is there. If not, look in other folders.

  3. Typo in the filename
    Git is case-sensitive. If the file is Table02.html or table2.html, it won’t match table02.html.

  4. File not staged yet
    If you’re trying something like:

    bash
    git checkout table02.html

    or

    bash
    git restore table02.html

    but the file was never tracked, Git has no version of it to restore.

✅ To fix it:

  • Run ls or dir (Windows) to confirm the exact file name.

  • If the file exists, use the correct spelling and path. Example:

    bash
    git add path/to/table02.html

  • If it doesn’t exist, you’ll need to create it or recover it from a previous commit.

Do you remember if table02.html existed in a past commit, or are you just creating it now?

You said:
how do I change terminal directory in r terminal
ChatGPT said:

In the R terminal (or R console), you don’t use cd like in bash — instead you use R’s own functions.

Here are the main ways:

  1. Change working directory

    r
    setwd("path/to/your/folder")

    Example on Windows:

    r
    setwd("C:/Users/YourName/Documents/project")

    Example on macOS/Linux:

    r
    setwd("/home/yourname/project")

  2. Check your current working directory

    r
    getwd()

  3. If you want to run shell commands inside R
    You can still use system commands with system() or ! (in RStudio terminal pane, not the R console). Example:

    r
    system("ls")   # macOS/Linux
system("dir")  # Windows

⚠️ Important: The R console working directory is separate from the RStudio terminal tab.

  • In the R console, use setwd().

  • In the terminal tab, it behaves like a normal shell (cd folder_name).

👉 Do you mean you’re inside the R console (where you run R commands), or inside the RStudio Terminal tab (which is a shell)?

You said:
in terminal tab shell
ChatGPT said:

Got it 👍 — if you’re in the RStudio terminal tab shell, that’s just a regular shell (bash on Mac/Linux, PowerShell/Command Prompt on Windows).

So you can change directory the same way you would in a normal terminal:

  • macOS/Linux

    bash
    cd /path/to/your/folder

  • Windows (Command Prompt)

    cmd
    cd C:\path\to\your\folder

  • Windows (PowerShell)

    powershell
    Set-Location "C:\path\to\your\folder"

    (you can also just use cd most of the time in PowerShell)

✅ Tip: To check where you are:

  • macOS/Linux → pwd

  • Windows → cd (or Get-Location in PowerShell)

Do you want me to show you how to quickly set the terminal directory to the same folder as your R project in RStudio?

You said:
fix code below so that "Generated output is below" is displayed on html <!doctype html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="utf-8" /> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" /> <title>Table 1: Overview of Reported Potential Liver Safety Findings</title> <style> :root{font-family:Segoe UI, Roboto, system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif;color:#222;} body{margin:0;padding:20px;background:#f7f7fb;} .container{max-width:1100px;margin:0 auto;background:#fff;border-radius:10px;padding:20px;box-shadow:0 6px 18px rgba(20,20,40,0.06);} h1{margin:0 0 6px;text-align:center;font-size:20px;} .subtitle{text-align:center;color:#444;margin-bottom:18px;font-size:14px;} /* code wrapper */ .code-wrapper{position:relative;border:1px solid #e6e6e6;border-radius:8px;background:#f2f2f2;overflow:hidden;} /* super light gray */ .code-viewport{ max-height:55vh; overflow:auto; padding:12px; font-family: "Fira Code", Consolas, monospace; font-size:13px; line-height:1.4; background:transparent; } .lines{min-width:min-content;} .line{display:flex; align-items:flex-start; gap:12px; white-space:pre;} .ln{width:60px; text-align:right; color:#666; user-select:none; flex:0 0 60px; padding-right:6px;} .content{flex:1 1 auto; white-space:pre; overflow:visible; color:#111;} /* Copy button */ .copy-btn{position:absolute; top:8px; right:8px; z-index:3; padding:6px 10px; border-radius:6px; border:0; background:#2563eb; color:#fff; cursor:pointer; font-weight:600;} .copy-btn:active{transform:translateY(1px);} /* image and buttons */ .image-container{ text-align:center; margin-top:16px; } .image-container img{ max-width:100%; height:auto; border-radius:6px; border:1px solid #ddd; display:inline-block; } .buttons{ text-align:center; margin-top:14px; } .buttons a.btn{ display:inline-block; margin:6px; padding:10px 18px; border-radius:8px; background:#2563eb; color:#fff; text-decoration:none; font-weight:600; } .buttons a.btn:hover{ background:#1e4db7; } footer{ text-align:center; font-size:13px; color:#666; margin-top:18px; } @media(max-width:700px){ .ln{width:48px; flex:0 0 48px; text-align:right;} .copy-btn{top:6px; right:6px;} } </style> </head> <body> <div class="container"> <h1>Table 1: Overview of Reported Potential Liver Safety Findings</h1> <div class="subtitle">Dataset used for the program is <strong>cadhy.Rdata</strong> from the <strong>random.cdisc.data</strong> library. Copy the code below to generate a table of reported potential liver safety findings.</div> <div class="code-wrapper" id="codeWrapper" aria-label="R program code area"> <button class="copy-btn" id="copyBtn">Copy Code</button> <div class="code-viewport" id="codeViewport" tabindex="0" aria-label="R program scrollable code"> <div class="lines" id="lines"></div> </div> </div> <p>Generated output is below:</p> <div class="image-container"> <img src="images/table01.png" alt="Table output (table01.png)"> </div> <div class="buttons"> <a class="btn" href="Table1.pdf" download>Download PDF</a> <a class="btn" href="table01op.html" target="_blank">Open HTML</a> </div> </div> <!-- Full R code included (unchanged, same as before) --> <script id="rawCode" type="text/plain"> #Table 1 #Install and upload appropriate packages. library(tidyverse) library(magrittr) library(tern) library(dplyr) library(rtables) library(r2rtf) library(formatters) #Set seed for reproducibility. set.seed(619) #Removed any NA values. Also, added a record sequence number via RUNNUM. adhy <- df_explicit_na(cadhy) cadhy2 <- adhy %>% add_column(RUNNUM=runif(nrow(.))) #Define values of interest in PARAMCD variable. paramcd_tbili_alt <- c("BLAL", "BGAL", "BA2AL", "BA5AL") paramcd_tbili_ast <- c("BLAS", "BGAS", "BA2AS", "BA5AS") param_extra <- c("BL2AL2CB", "BL2AL2CU", "BL2AS2CB", "BL2AS2CU") #Select laboratory test parameters. adhy_liver_tot <- cadhy2 %>% filter( SAFFL == "Y", AVISIT %in% c("POST-BASELINE"), PARAMCD %in% c(paramcd_tbili_alt, paramcd_tbili_ast, param_extra), AVAL %in% c(0, 1, 2, 3, 4) ) #ADaM Programmers should have already included a variable #similar to BIOCHEMREL & ADVREL that follow the metadata's criteria for findings related to biochemical tests & findings related to adverse events, respectively. adhy_liver2 <- cadhy2 %>% mutate(BIOCHEMREL = ifelse(.8 > RUNNUM & RUNNUM > .5, "Yes", "No")) %>% mutate(ADVREL = ifelse(.55 > RUNNUM & RUNNUM > .25, "Yes", "No")) %>% filter( SAFFL == "Y", AVISIT %in% c("POST-BASELINE"), PARAMCD %in% c(paramcd_tbili_alt, paramcd_tbili_ast, param_extra), AVAL %in% c(1, 2, 3, 4) ) #Add appropriate labels for final table output. adhy_liver3 <- adhy_liver2 %>% mutate(FINBOTH = with_label(BIOCHEMREL == "Yes" & ADVREL == "Yes", " Findings Related to Both\n Adverse Events and Biochemical\n Tests"), FINAEREL = with_label(ADVREL == "Yes", " Findings Related to Adverse\n Events"), FINBCTEST = with_label(BIOCHEMREL == "Yes", " Findings Related to\n Biochemical Tests") ) #Flag total number of findings without duplicates. adhy_liver4 <- adhy_liver3 %>%mutate(TOP = case_when(ADVREL == "Yes" & BIOCHEMREL == "No" & FINBOTH == "FALSE"~"Y", ADVREL == "No" & BIOCHEMREL == "Yes" & FINBOTH == "FALSE"~"Y", .default = "N")) %>% mutate(TABLESEQ = RUNNUM) %>% mutate(FINTOP = with_label(TOP == "Y", "Any Potential Liver Safety Findings")) %>% mutate(ROWSPL1 = case_when(FINTOP == "FALSE" | FINTOP == "TRUE" ~ " ")) #Rename the treatment arms. levels(adhy_liver4$ACTARMCD) <- c("T1", "PL", "T2") levels(adhy_liver4$ACTARM) <- c("T1\n n/N(%)", "PL\n n/N(%)", "T2\n n/N(%)") adhy_liver4$ACTARMCD <- factor(adhy_liver4$ACTARMCD,levels = c("PL", "T1", "T2")) aefin_vars <- c("FINBOTH", "FINAEREL", "FINBCTEST") #Create a custom format function. format_frac_perc <- function(x, output = NULL) {# x[1] is N, x[2] is the % (as decimal) perc <- round(x[2] * 100, 1) denom <- (x[1] / (x[2] * 100)) * 100 # Calculate denominator paste0(x[1],"/", denom, " (", perc, "%)") } #Build & render table's pseudo facet 1a. lyt_fin1a <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_rows_by(c("ROWSPL1")) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T1"), arm_y = c("PL"), col_label = paste0("\n", "Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", c("T1"), "-", c("PL"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_event( "TABLESEQ", filters = c("TOP"="Y"), denom = "N_col", riskdiff = TRUE, .labels = c(count_fraction = "Any Potential Liver Safety Findings"), .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc)) result_tabtop1a <- build_table(lyt_fin1a, df = adhy_liver4) #Build & render table's pseudo facet 1b. lyt_fin1b <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_rows_by(c("ROWSPL1")) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T2"), arm_y = c("PL", "T1"), col_label = paste0("Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", "T2-", c("PL", "T1"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_event( "TABLESEQ", filters = c("TOP"="Y"), denom = "N_col", riskdiff = TRUE, .labels = c(count_fraction = "Any Potential Liver Safety Findings"), .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc)) result_tabtop1b <- build_table(lyt_fin1b, df = adhy_liver4) #Bind specific columns from 2 top facets: 1a & 1b 1d. resulttopfin <- cbind_rtables(result_tabtop1a[, 1:ncol(result_tabtop1a)], result_tabtop1b[, 4:ncol(result_tabtop1b)]) #Constructing & rendering the bottom half of the table: facet 2a. lyt_fin2a <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T1"), arm_y = c("PL"), col_label = paste0("\n", "Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", c("T1"), "-", c("PL"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_flags( "RUNNUM", flag_variables = aefin_vars, denom = "N_col", var_labels = "", show_labels = "visible", riskdiff = TRUE, .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc), .indent_mods = 1 ) result_tabbot2a <- build_table(lyt_fin2a, df = adhy_liver4) #Constructing & rendering the bottom half of the table: facet 2b. lyt_fin2b <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T2"), arm_y = c("PL", "T1"), col_label = paste0("Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", "T2-", c("PL", "T1"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_flags( "RUNNUM", flag_variables = aefin_vars, denom = "N_col", var_labels = "", show_labels = "visible", riskdiff = TRUE, .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc), .indent_mods = ) result_tabbot2b <- build_table(lyt_fin2b, df = adhy_liver4) #Bind specific columns from all 4 facets: 2a & 2b. resultbot <- cbind_rtables(result_tabbot2a[, 1:ncol(result_tabbot2a)], result_tabbot2b[, 4:ncol(result_tabbot2b)]) resultbot #Bind specific rows from top & bottom halves of the table. resultfin <- rbind( resulttopfin[nrow(resulttopfin), ], resultbot[3:nrow(resultbot), ], resultbot[2, ] ) resultfin2 <- insert_rrow(resultfin, rrow("Number of Subjects with Liver\nSafety Findings(a)"), at = 1) main_title(resultfin2) <- paste("Table 1", "\nOverview of Reported Potential Liver Safety Findings", "\n<Analysis Set>") main_footer(resultfin2) <-paste("PL=Placebo, T1=Treatment 1, T2=Treatment 2", "\nNote (a): A potential liver safety finding is defined as meeting any of the laboratory thresholds.", "\nN = number of applicable subjects in treatment arm; n = actual applicable observed number with values.") #Output table as a PDF file. table1a2 <- "~/Table1.pdf" export_as_pdf(resultfin2, file = table1a2, landscape = TRUE, paginate = FALSE, colwidths = c(36, 14, 14, 14, 16, 16, 16))</script> <script> (function(){ let raw = document.getElementById('rawCode').textContent.replace(/\r/g, '').replace(/^\n+/, '').replace(/\n+$/, ''); const linesArray = raw.split('\n'); const linesContainer = document.getElementById('lines'); const frag = document.createDocumentFragment(); linesArray.forEach((text, idx) => { const row = document.createElement('div'); row.className='line'; const ln = document.createElement('div'); ln.className='ln'; ln.textContent = idx+1; const content = document.createElement('div'); content.className='content'; content.textContent=text; row.appendChild(ln); row.appendChild(content); frag.appendChild(row); }); linesContainer.appendChild(frag); document.getElementById('copyBtn').addEventListener('click', async ()=>{ try { await navigator.clipboard.writeText(raw); let b=document.getElementById('copyBtn'); b.textContent='Copied!'; setTimeout(()=>b.textContent='Copy Code',1200); } catch(e) { alert('Copy failed'); } }); // ❌ Removed the snapshot HTML generator here })(); </script>
ChatGPT said:

Thanks for sharing the full code — I see the issue.

Right now, your <p>Generated output is below:</p> is inside the HTML, but your JavaScript was trying to overwrite the whole container content (probably with a missing block in the snippet you posted).

The clean fix is simply to leave that <p> in the body (where you already put it) and make sure the script doesn’t remove or overwrite it.

Here’s a corrected minimal section of your HTML body where the text will display properly:

html
<div class="container">
  <h1>Table 1: Overview of Reported Potential Liver Safety Findings</h1>
  <div class="subtitle">
    Dataset used for the program is <strong>cadhy.Rdata</strong> from the 
    <strong>random.cdisc.data</strong> library. Copy the code below to generate a table of reported potential liver safety findings.
  </div>

  <div class="code-wrapper" id="codeWrapper" aria-label="R program code area">
    <button class="copy-btn" id="copyBtn">Copy Code</button>
    <div class="code-viewport" id="codeViewport" tabindex="0" aria-label="R program scrollable code">
      <div class="lines" id="lines"></div>
    </div>
  </div>

  <!-- ✅ This line will now display -->
  <p style="margin-top:20px;font-weight:600;">Generated output is below:</p>

  <div class="image-container">
    <img src="images/table01.png" alt="Table output (table01.png)">
  </div>

  <div class="buttons">
    <a class="btn" href="Table1.pdf" download>Download PDF</a>
    <a class="btn" href="table01op.html" target="_blank">Open HTML</a>
  </div>
</div>

What changed / why it works:

  • Your original <p> was fine — the problem was that some JavaScript was replacing the content of the container (removing that paragraph).

  • I added inline style for clarity (optional), but the important fix is don’t overwrite .container in JS. Your last script block (// ❌ Removed the snapshot HTML generator here) should stay exactly as-is, because that means nothing is overwriting your markup.

👉 If you paste this fix and reload, you should see "Generated output is below" appear just above your image.

Do you want me to adjust your script so it injects that line dynamically (instead of hardcoding <p> in HTML), or is it fine to keep it written directly in the HTML like this?

You said:
is this correct <!doctype html> <html lang="en"> <head> <meta charset="utf-8" /> <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1" /> <title>Table 1: Overview of Reported Potential Liver Safety Findings</title> <style> :root{font-family:Segoe UI, Roboto, system-ui, -apple-system, sans-serif;color:#222;} body{margin:0;padding:20px;background:#f7f7fb;} .container{max-width:1100px;margin:0 auto;background:#fff;border-radius:10px;padding:20px;box-shadow:0 6px 18px rgba(20,20,40,0.06);} h1{margin:0 0 6px;text-align:center;font-size:20px;} .subtitle{text-align:center;color:#444;margin-bottom:18px;font-size:14px;} /* code wrapper */ .code-wrapper{position:relative;border:1px solid #e6e6e6;border-radius:8px;background:#f2f2f2;overflow:hidden;} /* super light gray */ .code-viewport{ max-height:55vh; overflow:auto; padding:12px; font-family: "Fira Code", Consolas, monospace; font-size:13px; line-height:1.4; background:transparent; } .lines{min-width:min-content;} .line{display:flex; align-items:flex-start; gap:12px; white-space:pre;} .ln{width:60px; text-align:right; color:#666; user-select:none; flex:0 0 60px; padding-right:6px;} .content{flex:1 1 auto; white-space:pre; overflow:visible; color:#111;} /* Copy button */ .copy-btn{position:absolute; top:8px; right:8px; z-index:3; padding:6px 10px; border-radius:6px; border:0; background:#2563eb; color:#fff; cursor:pointer; font-weight:600;} .copy-btn:active{transform:translateY(1px);} /* image and buttons */ .image-container{ text-align:center; margin-top:16px; } .image-container img{ max-width:100%; height:auto; border-radius:6px; border:1px solid #ddd; display:inline-block; } .buttons{ text-align:center; margin-top:14px; } .buttons a.btn{ display:inline-block; margin:6px; padding:10px 18px; border-radius:8px; background:#2563eb; color:#fff; text-decoration:none; font-weight:600; } .buttons a.btn:hover{ background:#1e4db7; } footer{ text-align:center; font-size:13px; color:#666; margin-top:18px; } @media(max-width:700px){ .ln{width:48px; flex:0 0 48px; text-align:right;} .copy-btn{top:6px; right:6px;} } </style> </head> <body> <div class="container"> <h1>Table 1: Overview of Reported Potential Liver Safety Findings</h1> <div class="subtitle"> Dataset used for the program is <strong>cadhy.Rdata</strong> from the <strong>random.cdisc.data</strong> library. Copy the code below to generate a table of reported potential liver safety findings. </div> <div class="code-wrapper" id="codeWrapper" aria-label="R program code area"> <button class="copy-btn" id="copyBtn">Copy Code</button> <div class="code-viewport" id="codeViewport" tabindex="0" aria-label="R program scrollable code"> <div class="lines" id="lines"></div> </div> </div> <!-- ✅ This line will now display --> <p style="margin-top:20px;font-weight:600;">Generated output is below:</p> <div class="image-container"> <img src="images/table01.png" alt="Table output (table01.png)"> </div> <div class="buttons"> <a class="btn" href="Table1.pdf" download>Download PDF</a> <a class="btn" href="table01op.html" target="_blank">Open HTML</a> </div> </div> </div> <!-- Full R code included (unchanged, same as before) --> <script id="rawCode" type="text/plain"> #Table 1 #Install and upload appropriate packages. library(tidyverse) library(magrittr) library(tern) library(dplyr) library(rtables) library(r2rtf) library(formatters) #Set seed for reproducibility. set.seed(619) #Removed any NA values. Also, added a record sequence number via RUNNUM. adhy <- df_explicit_na(cadhy) cadhy2 <- adhy %>% add_column(RUNNUM=runif(nrow(.))) #Define values of interest in PARAMCD variable. paramcd_tbili_alt <- c("BLAL", "BGAL", "BA2AL", "BA5AL") paramcd_tbili_ast <- c("BLAS", "BGAS", "BA2AS", "BA5AS") param_extra <- c("BL2AL2CB", "BL2AL2CU", "BL2AS2CB", "BL2AS2CU") #Select laboratory test parameters. adhy_liver_tot <- cadhy2 %>% filter( SAFFL == "Y", AVISIT %in% c("POST-BASELINE"), PARAMCD %in% c(paramcd_tbili_alt, paramcd_tbili_ast, param_extra), AVAL %in% c(0, 1, 2, 3, 4) ) #ADaM Programmers should have already included a variable #similar to BIOCHEMREL & ADVREL that follow the metadata's criteria for findings related to biochemical tests & findings related to adverse events, respectively. adhy_liver2 <- cadhy2 %>% mutate(BIOCHEMREL = ifelse(.8 > RUNNUM & RUNNUM > .5, "Yes", "No")) %>% mutate(ADVREL = ifelse(.55 > RUNNUM & RUNNUM > .25, "Yes", "No")) %>% filter( SAFFL == "Y", AVISIT %in% c("POST-BASELINE"), PARAMCD %in% c(paramcd_tbili_alt, paramcd_tbili_ast, param_extra), AVAL %in% c(1, 2, 3, 4) ) #Add appropriate labels for final table output. adhy_liver3 <- adhy_liver2 %>% mutate(FINBOTH = with_label(BIOCHEMREL == "Yes" & ADVREL == "Yes", " Findings Related to Both\n Adverse Events and Biochemical\n Tests"), FINAEREL = with_label(ADVREL == "Yes", " Findings Related to Adverse\n Events"), FINBCTEST = with_label(BIOCHEMREL == "Yes", " Findings Related to\n Biochemical Tests") ) #Flag total number of findings without duplicates. adhy_liver4 <- adhy_liver3 %>%mutate(TOP = case_when(ADVREL == "Yes" & BIOCHEMREL == "No" & FINBOTH == "FALSE"~"Y", ADVREL == "No" & BIOCHEMREL == "Yes" & FINBOTH == "FALSE"~"Y", .default = "N")) %>% mutate(TABLESEQ = RUNNUM) %>% mutate(FINTOP = with_label(TOP == "Y", "Any Potential Liver Safety Findings")) %>% mutate(ROWSPL1 = case_when(FINTOP == "FALSE" | FINTOP == "TRUE" ~ " ")) #Rename the treatment arms. levels(adhy_liver4$ACTARMCD) <- c("T1", "PL", "T2") levels(adhy_liver4$ACTARM) <- c("T1\n n/N(%)", "PL\n n/N(%)", "T2\n n/N(%)") adhy_liver4$ACTARMCD <- factor(adhy_liver4$ACTARMCD,levels = c("PL", "T1", "T2")) aefin_vars <- c("FINBOTH", "FINAEREL", "FINBCTEST") #Create a custom format function. format_frac_perc <- function(x, output = NULL) {# x[1] is N, x[2] is the % (as decimal) perc <- round(x[2] * 100, 1) denom <- (x[1] / (x[2] * 100)) * 100 # Calculate denominator paste0(x[1],"/", denom, " (", perc, "%)") } #Build & render table's pseudo facet 1a. lyt_fin1a <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_rows_by(c("ROWSPL1")) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T1"), arm_y = c("PL"), col_label = paste0("\n", "Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", c("T1"), "-", c("PL"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_event( "TABLESEQ", filters = c("TOP"="Y"), denom = "N_col", riskdiff = TRUE, .labels = c(count_fraction = "Any Potential Liver Safety Findings"), .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc)) result_tabtop1a <- build_table(lyt_fin1a, df = adhy_liver4) #Build & render table's pseudo facet 1b. lyt_fin1b <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_rows_by(c("ROWSPL1")) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T2"), arm_y = c("PL", "T1"), col_label = paste0("Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", "T2-", c("PL", "T1"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_event( "TABLESEQ", filters = c("TOP"="Y"), denom = "N_col", riskdiff = TRUE, .labels = c(count_fraction = "Any Potential Liver Safety Findings"), .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc)) result_tabtop1b <- build_table(lyt_fin1b, df = adhy_liver4) #Bind specific columns from 2 top facets: 1a & 1b 1d. resulttopfin <- cbind_rtables(result_tabtop1a[, 1:ncol(result_tabtop1a)], result_tabtop1b[, 4:ncol(result_tabtop1b)]) #Constructing & rendering the bottom half of the table: facet 2a. lyt_fin2a <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T1"), arm_y = c("PL"), col_label = paste0("\n", "Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", c("T1"), "-", c("PL"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_flags( "RUNNUM", flag_variables = aefin_vars, denom = "N_col", var_labels = "", show_labels = "visible", riskdiff = TRUE, .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc), .indent_mods = 1 ) result_tabbot2a <- build_table(lyt_fin2a, df = adhy_liver4) #Constructing & rendering the bottom half of the table: facet 2b. lyt_fin2b <- basic_table(show_colcounts = TRUE) %>% split_cols_by("ACTARMCD", show_colcounts = TRUE, labels_var = "ACTARM", split_fun = add_riskdiff(arm_x = c("T2"), arm_y = c("PL", "T1"), col_label = paste0("Risk", "\n", "Difference", "\n", "T2-", c("PL", "T1"), "\n", "(95% CI)"))) %>% count_patients_with_flags( "RUNNUM", flag_variables = aefin_vars, denom = "N_col", var_labels = "", show_labels = "visible", riskdiff = TRUE, .formats = c(count_fraction = format_frac_perc), .indent_mods = ) result_tabbot2b <- build_table(lyt_fin2b, df = adhy_liver4) #Bind specific columns from all 4 facets: 2a & 2b. resultbot <- cbind_rtables(result_tabbot2a[, 1:ncol(result_tabbot2a)], result_tabbot2b[, 4:ncol(result_tabbot2b)]) resultbot #Bind specific rows from top & bottom halves of the table. resultfin <- rbind( resulttopfin[nrow(resulttopfin), ], resultbot[3:nrow(resultbot), ], resultbot[2, ] ) resultfin2 <- insert_rrow(resultfin, rrow("Number of Subjects with Liver\nSafety Findings(a)"), at = 1) main_title(resultfin2) <- paste("Table 1", "\nOverview of Reported Potential Liver Safety Findings", "\n<Analysis Set>") main_footer(resultfin2) <-paste("PL=Placebo, T1=Treatment 1, T2=Treatment 2", "\nNote (a): A potential liver safety finding is defined as meeting any of the laboratory thresholds.", "\nN = number of applicable subjects in treatment arm; n = actual applicable observed number with values.") #Output table as a PDF file. table1a2 <- "~/Table1.pdf" export_as_pdf(resultfin2, file = table1a2, landscape = TRUE, paginate = FALSE, colwidths = c(36, 14, 14, 14, 16, 16, 16))</script> <script> (function(){ let raw = document.getElementById('rawCode').textContent.replace(/\r/g, '').replace(/^\n+/, '').replace(/\n+$/, ''); const linesArray = raw.split('\n'); const linesContainer = document.getElementById('lines'); const frag = document.createDocumentFragment(); linesArray.forEach((text, idx) => { const row = document.createElement('div'); row.className='line'; const ln = document.createElement('div'); ln.className='ln'; ln.textContent = idx+1; const content = document.createElement('div'); content.className='content'; content.textContent=text; row.appendChild(ln); row.appendChild(content); frag.appendChild(row); }); linesContainer.appendChild(frag); document.getElementById('copyBtn').addEventListener('click', async ()=>{ try { await navigator.clipboard.writeText(raw); let b=document.getElementById('copyBtn'); b.textContent='Copied!'; setTimeout(()=>b.textContent='Copy Code',1200); } catch(e) { alert('Copy failed'); } }); // ❌ Removed the snapshot HTML generator here })(); </script>
ChatGPT said:

You’re very close 👍 but I spotted two issues in what you pasted:

  1. Extra closing </div>
    After your .container block ends, you have one extra </div>. This will break the HTML structure.

    html
</div> <!-- closes .container --> </div> <!-- ❌ this one should be removed --> ```

You only need the first one.

  1. R code typo in your <script id="rawCode"> block
    Inside your R code you have lines like:

    r

paramcd_tbili_alt <- c("BLAL", "BGAL", "BA2AL", "BA5AL") paramcd_tbili_ast
<- c("BLAS", "BGAS", "BA2AS", "BA5AS")

scss

That’s invalid R because the second assignment is stuck on the same line.  
It should be:

```r
paramcd_tbili_alt <- c("BLAL", "BGAL", "BA2AL", "BA5AL") 
paramcd_tbili_ast <- c("BLAS", "BGAS", "BA2AS", "BA5AS")

(Otherwise, R will throw a parse error if someone copies it out.)

✅ Corrected snippet (fixed the extra </div> and R code line split):

html
  <div class="buttons">
    <a class="btn" href="Table1.pdf" download>Download PDF</a>
    <a class="btn" href="table01op.html" target="_blank">Open HTML</a>
  </div>
</div> <!-- closes .container -->

r
#Define values of interest in PARAMCD variable.
paramcd_tbili_alt <- c("BLAL", "BGAL", "BA2AL", "BA5AL") 
paramcd_tbili_ast <- c("BLAS", "BGAS", "BA2AS", "BA5AS")
param_extra <- c("BL2AL2CB", "BL2AL2CU", "BL2AS2CB", "BL2AS2CU")

With those two fixes, your HTML is valid and the line
“Generated output is below:” will display correctly.

👉 Do you want me to also validate the entire HTML structure (head/body nesting, accessibility, etc.) to make sure it’s production-ready, or are you mainly focused on getting it to render and show that text?

You said:
picture is not showing pu in html
ChatGPT said:

I see what’s happening 👀 — the image placeholder <img> is there, but the browser can’t actually load table02.png.

Here are the most common causes and fixes:

1. File not in the same folder as your HTML

  • Your HTML is at:

    swift
    C:/Users/Dimple/Documents/hepatotox/hepatotox-site/docs/table02.html

  • The <img> tag is:

    html
    <img src="table02.png" alt="Table output (table02.png)">

  • That means the browser is looking for the image at:

    swift
    C:/Users/Dimple/Documents/hepatotox/hepatotox-site/docs/table02.png

✅ Check if table02.png is really in the same docs folder as your table02.html.

  • If not, move it there, or adjust the path.

2. Wrong relative path

  • If your image is in a subfolder like docs/images/, update the HTML:

    html
    <img src="images/table02.png" alt="Table output (table02.png)">

  • If it’s one level up, use:

    html
    <img src="../table02.png" alt="Table output (table02.png)">

3. Filename mismatch

  • Windows isn’t case-sensitive, but browsers are stricter, especially when hosted.
    Make sure it’s exactly table02.png and not Table02.PNG or table2.png.

4. Image missing entirely

  • Double-check the file actually exists in your project. If not, export or save the plot/table again as table02.png.

👉 Quick test: Open table02.png directly in your browser by typing its full path:

perl
file:///C:/Users/Dimple/Documents/hepatotox/hepatotox-site/docs/table02.png

If it doesn’t load, the file isn’t there (or name is off).

Do you want me to help rewrite the <img> path correctly if you tell me where your table02.png is currently saved?

You said:
picture is not showing up in the git hub pages
ChatGPT said:

Ah — now I see the issue clearly 👍

On your local machine, table02.png shows (if in the right folder), but on GitHub Pages it doesn’t load. That usually means one of these:

🔍 Common causes on GitHub Pages

  1. File not pushed to repo

    • Did you commit & push table02.png to your GitHub repo?

    • Run:

      bash
      git status

      If you see table02.png under "untracked files", it never made it up.
      Add & push it:

      bash
      git add docs/table02.png
git commit -m "Add table02.png"
git push

  2. Wrong path in HTML

    • Your HTML is at:

      arduino
      https://phuse-org.github.io/hepatotox/table02.html

    • That means the browser looks for:

      arduino
      https://phuse-org.github.io/hepatotox/table02.png

    • ✅ Check if that URL works directly in the browser.

      • If it 404s, then either the image isn’t in the repo or is in a different folder.

    • If you stored it in docs/images/table02.png, then update HTML:

      html
      <img src="images/table02.png" alt="Table output (table02.png)">

  3. Case sensitivity

    • GitHub Pages is case-sensitive.

    • Table02.png ≠ table02.png.

    • Double-check exact filename in your repo.

✅ How to debug

  1. Go to your repo on GitHub → navigate to docs/ → confirm that table02.png exists.

  2. Copy the raw URL of the PNG and test it in the browser.

  3. Adjust the <img src="..."> in your HTML until it matches exactly.

⚡ Quick fix example:
If your repo structure is:

markdown
docs/
  table02.html
  images/
    table02.png

Then your HTML should be:

html
<img src="images/table02.png" alt="Table output (table02.png)">

👉 Do you want to paste me the exact repo file structure for docs/ (just the files/folders), so I can give you the correct <img src> path?

You said:
$ git push fatal: The current branch main has no upstream branch. To push the current branch and set the remote as upstream, use
ChatGPT said:

That error means your local branch main isn’t linked to the GitHub remote yet. Git is telling you that it doesn’t know where to push.

You can fix it with one of these:

1. If your GitHub repo is already created and remote is set

Run:

bash
git push -u origin main

  • -u sets origin/main as the upstream, so next time you can just use git push.

2. If you haven’t set the remote yet

First add the GitHub repo URL:

bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-repo>.git

Then push and set upstream:

bash
git push -u origin main

3. To check what remotes you have

bash
git remote -v

This will show if origin is already pointing to your GitHub repo.

✅ After this, git push and git pull will work normally without extra flags.

Do you want me to walk you through checking your remote URL first, to make sure you’re pushing to the right GitHub repo (the one that serves your GitHub Pages site)?

You said:
convert pdf to html file
ChatGPT said:

Got it 👍 — there are a few ways to convert a PDF into an HTML file, depending on what you need (text only, text + images, or exact formatting).

🔹 Option 1: Quick conversion with pypandoc (text + basic formatting)

If you want a clean HTML with text preserved:

bash
pip install pypandoc

Then run:

python
import pypandoc

output = pypandoc.convert_file("Table1.pdf", "html", outputfile="table1.html", extra_args=["--standalone"])
print("Converted -> table1.html")

This gives you an HTML file with your PDF text + structure.

🔹 Option 2: With pdf2image + img tag (snapshot style)

If you only need to show the PDF as an image in your webpage (like your table outputs), you can rasterize it:

bash
pip install pdf2image pillow

python
from pdf2image import convert_from_path

# Convert PDF pages to PNGs
pages = convert_from_path("Table1.pdf")
for i, page in enumerate(pages):
    page.save(f"table1_page{i+1}.png", "PNG")

print("Saved images: table1_page1.png ...")

Then in your HTML:

html
<img src="table1_page1.png" alt="Table 1 output">

🔹 Option 3: Exact formatting (CSS + text overlay)

If you want to preserve layout + fonts exactly, tools like pdf2htmlEX are best:

bash
sudo apt-get install pdf2htmlex   # Linux
pdf2htmlex Table1.pdf table1.html

This keeps fonts, alignment, tables, etc. but the HTML is harder to edit.

👉 Question for you: do you want your HTML page to actually contain selectable text from the PDF, or is it enough to just show the PDF content as an image (like your table01.png and table02.png examples)?

You said:
can you convert w selectable text with exact layout and font
ChatGPT said:

Yes ✅ — to get selectable text + exact layout and fonts, the best tool is pdf2htmlEX. Unlike simple converters, it embeds fonts, preserves styles with CSS, and makes the HTML look exactly like your PDF, but still selectable and copyable.

🔹 Install pdf2htmlEX

  • Linux / WSL:

    bash
    sudo apt-get install pdf2htmlex

  • Mac (Homebrew):

    bash
    brew install pdf2htmlex

  • Windows:
    Prebuilt binaries exist, or you can install via Chocolatey:

    powershell
    choco install pdf2htmlex

🔹 Convert your PDF

Run:

bash
pdf2htmlex Table1.pdf table1.html

This will generate:

  • table1.html → the HTML file with selectable text.

  • A table1_files/ folder → contains fonts, CSS, and background images if needed.

🔹 Host on GitHub Pages

Make sure you commit both the .html file and the _files folder to your repo, otherwise fonts and CSS won’t load.
Your project structure will look like:

css
docs/
  table1.html
  table1_files/
    fonts.css
    font.ttf
    bg.png

Then your GitHub Pages site can open table1.html with full formatting + selectable text.

⚠️ Note: pdf2htmlEX preserves layout by using absolute positioning (lots of <div style="...">), so editing the HTML later is hard. But it’s perfect if you just want to view your PDF in-browser.

👉 Do you want me to give you the Windows step-by-step setup (since your screenshot shows Windows), so you can run this directly on your laptop?

You said:
no can I just upload the pdf and you convert it
ChatGPT said:

I can’t directly run pdf2htmlEX in this environment, so I can’t fully preserve fonts and layout myself here 😕.

But I can help you in two ways:

  1. ✅ If you upload your PDF, I can extract its text + structure into an HTML file (good for selectable text, but formatting will be basic).

  2. ⚡ If you need the exact layout and fonts preserved (like a true PDF viewer), that requires pdf2htmlEX or a similar tool run on your machine — because it embeds fonts and positions text precisely.

👉 If you upload your Table1.pdf here, do you want me to:

  • Convert it into a clean HTML file with selectable text,

  • Or prepare a ready-to-run command/script that you can use on Windows to get the exact PDF-to-HTML conversion with fonts/layout?

ChatGPT said:

Perfect 👍 — if you don’t need exact fonts preserved, I can convert your PDF into an HTML file with selectable text (clean, lightweight, easy to host on GitHub Pages).

If you upload your Table1.pdf here, I can:

  1. Extract the text from it,

  2. Wrap it in a simple HTML structure,

  3. Give you the .html file you can drop straight into your repo.

Do you want me to go ahead and do that if you upload the PDF?

You can download it here:
